metadata.description: Literature and literary studies skills with prerequisite ordering, organized by area of study

nodes.label: What Is Literature
nodes.description: Defining literature: aesthetic use of language, the literary canon, high vs. popular culture debates, and why we study literature.

nodes.label: Reading Strategies
nodes.description: Active reading techniques: previewing, questioning, summarizing, making connections, and monitoring comprehension.

nodes.label: Close Reading
nodes.description: Careful, sustained analysis of a short passage: attending to word choice, syntax, imagery, tone, and formal features.

nodes.label: Annotation
nodes.description: Marking up texts: highlighting, marginal notes, coding systems, and building a dialogue with the text on the page.

nodes.label: Literary vs. Genre Fiction
nodes.description: Distinctions and overlaps between literary fiction and genre fiction (mystery, sci-fi, romance). Marketing categories vs. artistic merit.

nodes.label: Plot Structure
nodes.description: Freytag's pyramid: exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, denouement. Variations in non-linear and episodic narratives.

nodes.label: Character Types
nodes.description: Protagonist, antagonist, foil, stock characters. Round vs. flat, static vs. dynamic characters and their narrative functions.

nodes.label: Point of View
nodes.description: First person, second person, third person limited and omniscient. Narrative distance, reliability, and focalization.

nodes.label: Setting
nodes.description: Time, place, and social environment in narrative. How setting shapes mood, constrains characters, and carries thematic weight.

nodes.label: Theme
nodes.description: Central ideas and underlying meanings in a literary work. Distinguishing theme from subject, and how theme emerges from narrative elements.

nodes.label: Symbolism
nodes.description: Objects, characters, or events that represent abstract ideas beyond their literal meaning. Conventional vs. literary symbols.

nodes.label: Motif
nodes.description: Recurring elements (images, phrases, situations) that develop or reinforce a theme throughout a literary work.

nodes.label: Irony
nodes.description: Verbal, situational, and dramatic irony. How the gap between appearance and reality creates meaning, humor, or pathos.

nodes.label: Foreshadowing
nodes.description: Hints or clues planted early in a narrative that anticipate later events. Direct, indirect, and symbolic foreshadowing techniques.

nodes.label: Meter
nodes.description: Rhythmic patterns in poetry: iamb, trochee, anapest, dactyl, spondee. Scansion and how meter creates musical effects.

nodes.label: Rhyme
nodes.description: End rhyme, internal rhyme, slant rhyme, and rhyme schemes (ABAB, ABBA, couplets). How rhyme creates structure and emphasis.

nodes.label: Free Verse
nodes.description: Poetry without regular meter or rhyme scheme. Line breaks, white space, and organic form as structural elements.

nodes.label: Sonnets
nodes.description: Petrarchan (Italian) and Shakespearean (English) sonnet forms: 14 lines, volta, and the interplay of structure and argument.

nodes.label: Haiku
nodes.description: Japanese poetic form: 5-7-5 syllable structure, seasonal references (kigo), cutting words (kireji), and capturing a moment in nature.

nodes.label: Imagery
nodes.description: Sensory language that appeals to sight, sound, taste, touch, and smell. Creating vivid mental pictures through concrete detail.

nodes.label: Figurative Language
nodes.description: Metaphor, simile, personification, hyperbole, metonymy, synecdoche, and other figures of speech that create layered meaning.

nodes.label: Enjambment
nodes.description: Running a sentence or phrase across line breaks without punctuation. Creating tension, momentum, and surprise in poetry.

nodes.label: Poetic Voice
nodes.description: The speaker's persona, tone, and attitude in a poem. Distinguishing the speaker from the poet, and how voice shapes meaning.

nodes.label: Major Poets
nodes.description: Key figures across traditions: Shakespeare, Dickinson, Whitman, Keats, Neruda, Rumi, Bishop, Hughes, Plath, Walcott, and their contributions.

nodes.label: Short Story
nodes.description: The short story form: compression, unity of effect (Poe), epiphany (Joyce), and structural conventions from Chekhov to the present.

nodes.label: The Novel
nodes.description: Long-form prose fiction: origins (Cervantes, Defoe), the bildungsroman, epic scope, and the novel as a flexible, evolving form.

nodes.label: Novella
nodes.description: Mid-length fiction between story and novel: concentrated focus, limited cast, single central conflict. Conrad, Kafka, Steinbeck.

nodes.label: Flash Fiction
nodes.description: Extremely short fiction (under 1000 words): micro-narratives, sudden fiction, implication, and economy of language.

nodes.label: Narrative Techniques
nodes.description: Frame narratives, epistolary form, in medias res, non-linear chronology, multiple perspectives, and metafiction.

nodes.label: Stream of Consciousness
nodes.description: Interior monologue that mimics the flow of thought: Joyce, Woolf, Faulkner. Free association, fragmented syntax, and psychological depth.

nodes.label: Unreliable Narrator
nodes.description: Narrators whose credibility is compromised: intentional deception, naivety, or mental instability. Reader as detective.

nodes.label: Magical Realism
nodes.description: The matter-of-fact incorporation of fantastical elements into realistic fiction. García Márquez, Allende, Rushdie, Okri.

nodes.label: Tragedy
nodes.description: From Greek tragedy to modern: hamartia, catharsis, the fall of the great. Sophocles, Shakespeare, Miller, and the tragic vision.

nodes.label: Comedy
nodes.description: Comic forms from Aristophanes to sitcom: satire, farce, romantic comedy, comedy of manners, and the social function of laughter.

nodes.label: Tragicomedy
nodes.description: Blending tragic and comic elements: Chekhov, Beckett, Stoppard. The ambiguity of tone and the complexity of human experience.

nodes.label: Theatre of the Absurd
nodes.description: Beckett, Ionesco, Pinter: meaninglessness, circular dialogue, breakdown of logic, and existentialist themes on stage.

nodes.label: Aristotle's Poetics
nodes.description: The foundational text of dramatic theory: mimesis, plot as soul of tragedy, unity of action, recognition, reversal, and catharsis.

nodes.label: Shakespearean Drama
nodes.description: Shakespeare's tragedies, comedies, histories, and romances. Blank verse, soliloquy, dramatic structure, and enduring influence.

nodes.label: Modern Drama
nodes.description: Ibsen, Chekhov, Brecht, Williams, Miller: realism, epic theatre, the well-made play, and drama as social commentary.

nodes.label: Stage Directions
nodes.description: Reading and interpreting stage directions: blocking, set descriptions, character entrances/exits, and authorial intent in dramatic texts.

nodes.label: Dramatic Irony
nodes.description: When the audience knows something characters do not: creating suspense, pathos, and engagement across dramatic and narrative forms.

nodes.label: Personal Essay
nodes.description: The essay as exploration of self: Montaigne's tradition, personal voice, reflection, and the essayist's contract with the reader.

nodes.label: Literary Criticism (Writing)
nodes.description: Writing analytical essays about literature: thesis development, textual evidence, argument structure, and scholarly conventions.

nodes.label: Memoir
nodes.description: Life writing focused on specific periods or themes: memory, truth, narrative shaping of experience, and the memoir boom.

nodes.label: Biography
nodes.description: Writing the lives of others: research, narrative structure, ethical considerations, and the relationship between life and work.

nodes.label: New Journalism
nodes.description: Literary non-fiction: Wolfe, Didion, Capote, Mailer. Applying novelistic techniques to reportage and blurring fact/fiction boundaries.

nodes.label: Essay Forms
nodes.description: Lyric essay, hermit crab essay, braided essay, and other experimental non-fiction forms that push the boundaries of the genre.

nodes.label: Formalism
nodes.description: New Criticism and Russian Formalism: the text as self-contained object, defamiliarization, close reading as methodology.

nodes.label: Structuralism
nodes.description: Saussure, Lévi-Strauss, Barthes: underlying systems and codes, binary oppositions, narrative grammar, and deep structures of meaning.

nodes.label: Post-Structuralism
nodes.description: Derrida, Foucault, Barthes (late): instability of meaning, the death of the author, power/knowledge, and textual play.

nodes.label: Marxist Criticism
nodes.description: Literature and class: ideology, base/superstructure, cultural hegemony (Gramsci), and the Frankfurt School on culture industry.

nodes.label: Feminist Criticism
nodes.description: Gender and literature: Woolf, Beauvoir, Showalter, Gilbert & Gubar. Recovering women's writing and analyzing gender representation.

nodes.label: Postcolonial Criticism
nodes.description: Said's Orientalism, Spivak's subaltern, Bhabha's hybridity: literature, empire, identity, and writing back to the center.

nodes.label: Psychoanalytic Criticism
nodes.description: Freud, Lacan, and literature: the unconscious, desire, repression, the mirror stage, and reading texts as dreams or symptoms.

nodes.label: Reader-Response Theory
nodes.description: Iser, Fish, Jauss: meaning as co-created by reader and text, interpretive communities, horizons of expectation, and reception aesthetics.

nodes.label: Deconstruction
nodes.description: Derrida's method: revealing internal contradictions in texts, differance, logocentrism, and the undecidability of meaning.

nodes.label: New Historicism
nodes.description: Greenblatt and the circulation of social energy: literature as embedded in historical context, anecdotes, and cultural negotiations.

nodes.label: Classical Literature (Greek & Roman)
nodes.description: Homer, Virgil, Ovid, Sappho, Greek drama. Epic, lyric, and dramatic foundations of the Western literary tradition.

nodes.label: Medieval Literature
nodes.description: Beowulf, Dante, Chaucer, The Tale of Genji. Allegory, romance, religious writing, and courtly love traditions.

nodes.label: Renaissance Literature
nodes.description: Shakespeare, Cervantes, Montaigne, Milton. Humanism, the printing press, vernacular literature, and the birth of the modern self.

nodes.label: Romantic Literature
nodes.description: Wordsworth, Coleridge, Shelley, Keats, Byron, Goethe. Imagination, nature, the sublime, individualism, and emotional intensity.

nodes.label: Victorian Literature
nodes.description: Dickens, Brontës, Eliot, Hardy, Tennyson. Social realism, the condition-of-England novel, empire, and moral earnestness.

nodes.label: Modernism
nodes.description: Joyce, Woolf, Eliot, Kafka, Faulkner. Fragmentation, stream of consciousness, mythic method, and the crisis of representation.

nodes.label: Postmodernism
nodes.description: Pynchon, Borges, DeLillo, Calvino. Metafiction, pastiche, irony, historiographic metafiction, and the collapse of grand narratives.

nodes.label: Latin American Boom
nodes.description: García Márquez, Cortázar, Vargas Llosa, Fuentes. Magical realism, political engagement, and the explosion of Latin American fiction in the 1960s–70s.

nodes.label: African Literature
nodes.description: Achebe, Ngũgĩ, Soyinka, Adichie. Decolonization, language politics, oral tradition's influence, and pan-African literary movements.

nodes.label: Asian Literature
nodes.description: Classical Chinese poetry, Japanese aesthetics (mono no aware), Indian epics, modern Asian fiction: Murakami, Tagore, Lu Xun, Mishima.

nodes.label: Oral Traditions
nodes.description: Epic recitation, griots, oral formulaic theory (Parry-Lord), folklore, mythology, and the transition from oral to written literature.
